title: Dog Vomiting Green Substance: Emergency Triage and Causes
meta_desc: Understand why your dog is vomiting green, when to seek emergency care, and how to help your vet. Urgency Level: High.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green vomit often indicates bile or grass consumption, but it can signal life-threatening issues like blockages or toxicity. Seek emergency veterinary care if your dog is lethargic, in pain, or repeatedly retching.

What does it mean if my dog is vomiting green liquid?

Green vomit is typically a sign that your dog is expelling bile, a digestive fluid produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder. Bile is released into the small intestine to aid in digestion, but it can back up into the stomach, causing irritation and vomiting. In other cases, the green color may simply be a result of your dog eating grass or plants.

Is green vomit in dogs considered a medical emergency?

  • Treat green vomiting as a potential emergency (Urgency Level: High).
  • Recognize that it can be a primary symptom of serious conditions such as gastrointestinal blockages, heatstroke, or toxic ingestion.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your dog is also showing signs of lethargy, abdominal pain, or repeated retching.

Why should I take a photo of my dog's vomit for the veterinarian?

  • Take a clear photo of the vomit before cleaning it up to provide vital visual information.
  • Allow the veterinary triage team to assess the consistency, the specific shade of green, and the presence of any foreign materials or blood.
  • Provide data that can significantly speed up the diagnostic process and help the medical team determine the severity of your dog's condition.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, vomiting of sudden onset may contain bile, food, froth, blood (frank or digested), or evidence of an ingested substance such as grass, bones, or foreign material. Potential causes include dietary indiscretion or intolerance (e.g., ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods or foreign material), drug or toxin ingestion (e.g., antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illness (e.g., pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, hypoadrenocorticism), endoparasitism (e.g., Physaloptera sp), or bacterial (e.g., Helicobacter-associated disease) or viral infection (e.g., canine parvovirus gastroenteritis). Initial management involves withholding food for a short period, feeding an easily digested diet, and offering small amounts of water frequently. Careful monitoring for persistent vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, and/or fever is critical; if vomiting persists, reevaluation is warranted, including abdominal palpation to assess for foreign body or abdominal discomfort. Abdominal radiographs should be performed to evaluate for radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal distention, potentially indicating obstruction.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: Can grass make my dog's vomit look green?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your dog consumes a large amount of grass, the chlorophyll will stain the gastric fluid green.

faqs.question: What if my dog is acting normal otherwise?
faqs.answer: Even if your dog seems okay, persistent green vomiting is abnormal. It is best to call your vet to rule out underlying issues like pancreatitis or early-stage obstruction.

faqs.question: Should I feed my dog after they vomit green?
faqs.answer: Do not offer food until you have spoken with a professional. Feeding a dog with a potential blockage or upset stomach can worsen the situation.
